response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As an 85-year-old man from Naples who has seen many changes in my lifetime, I approach social media with a mix of curiosity and caution. Having no formal education but plenty of life experience, I've learned that not everything you hear or read is true, whether it's gossip in the piazza or posts online. I use social media a few times weekly to stay connected with family and see what's happening in the world, but I've noticed more and more fake content that worries me. My Christian faith teaches me to value truth and honesty, so when I see false information spreading, it feels like a betrayal of those values. I'm naturally curious, which helps me question what I see, but my high neuroticism means fake news can make me anxious, especially when it's about health or safety. Living in Naples, I've seen how rumors can spread quickly and cause real harm, so I'm careful about what I share. When I come across something suspicious, I try to check it with reliable sources, like trusted news programs or people I know who are knowledgeable. My hobbies like painting and cycling keep me grounded in the real world, reminding me that not everything important happens online. I think the key to dealing with fake content is to slow down, not believe everything at first glance, and remember that social media is just one part of life. It's also important to talk about these issues with others, especially younger people who might be more immersed in these platforms. While I enjoy the connection social media provides, I've learned to trust my instincts and life experience to navigate its challenges.",

Psychological feature curious supports questioning fake content. Religious beliefs Christianity emphasizes truth values. Time on social media few times weekly shows moderate engagement. Hobbies painting, rock climbing, videogames, cycling suggest diverse interests balancing online/offline. OCEAN: openness moderate (40) allows some adaptability, conscientiousness low (10) might mean less systematic approach, extraversion moderate (46) indicates social but not dominant, agreeableness moderate (53) shows balanced skepticism, neuroticism high (71) explains anxiety about fake content. Tone crafted as reflective, cautious, grounded in personal experience rather than technical solutions, with Neapolitan cultural flavor.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As an 85-year-old man from Naples who has seen many changes in my lifetime, I approach social media with a mix of curiosity and caution. Having no formal education but plenty of life experience, I've learned that not everything you hear or read is true, whether it's gossip in the piazza or posts online. I use social media a few times weekly to stay connected with family and see what's happening in the world, but I've noticed more and more fake content that worries me. My Christian faith teaches me to value truth and honesty, so when I see false information spreading, it feels like a betrayal of those values. I'm naturally curious, which helps me question what I see, but my high neuroticism means fake news can make me anxious, especially when it's about health or safety. Living in Naples, I've seen how rumors can spread quickly and cause real harm, so I'm careful about what I share. When I come across something suspicious, I try to check it with reliable sources, like trusted news programs or people I know who are knowledgeable. My hobbies like painting and cycling keep me grounded in the real world, reminding me that not everything important happens online. I think the key to dealing with fake content is to slow down, not believe everything at first glance, and remember that social media is just one part of life. It's also important to talk about these issues with others, especially younger people who might be more immersed in these platforms. While I enjoy the connection social media provides, I've learned to trust my instincts and life experience to navigate its challenges.
